title: Unconventional deterrence: How Europe can ward off a Russian attack – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Jean-Marie Guéhenno argues that nuclear deterrence alone does not preclude major war — as Russia and Israel have both been struck despite being nuclear powers — and that Europe must build a 'continuum of deterrence' combining French/British nuclear doctrine, the US umbrella, and much stronger conventional long-range strike capability, because a weakening Putin is likely to test NATO territory with hybrid and conventional attacks below the nuclear threshold.

insights.claim: In a March 2026 speech on nuclear posture, Emmanuel Macron floated 'forward deterrence' — France deploying nuclear bombs elsewhere on the continent after consultation with European partners — a notable opening given France has historically refused to join NATO's nuclear planning group.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: A real, if largely symbolic, shift in French nuclear posture toward continental burden-sharing — worth monitoring for follow-through on actual basing/consultation mechanisms.
insights.locator: In March, France’s president Emmanuel Macron gave a speech

insights.claim: France's nuclear arsenal is explicitly unsuited for 'tactical' escalation: Macron reaffirmed France rejects any tactical use of nuclear weapons, and its smaller nuclear weapons function only as a final warning shot before strategic strikes, not as a rung on an escalation ladder.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: Clarifies that French nuclear deterrence cannot substitute for graduated conventional response — implying the actual capability gap Europe must fill is conventional, not nuclear.
insights.locator: But Macron also affirmed that France continues to reject

insights.claim: It is implausible that a French president would be the first to cross the nuclear threshold to defend an overrun Baltic state, even under 'forward deterrence,' but Guéhenno warns extreme-right parties will likely misrepresent forward deterrence's real implications for political gain.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: Warns that forward deterrence is more valuable as political signalling than as an operational guarantee — investors/policy-watchers should discount claims that it materially changes Baltic-state security.
insights.locator: Although France, contrary to the US, is directly affected

insights.claim: Forward deterrence's main value is political — asserting that French vital interests extend beyond French territory — but Guéhenno cautions it changes little in practice and risks becoming another way for Europeans to avoid confronting the real requirements of war readiness, much as reliance on the US nuclear umbrella has smothered European strategic thinking.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: A caution against over-reading French nuclear gestures as a substitute for the harder, costlier build-out of conventional deterrence capacity.
insights.locator: It would thus be unfortunate if the idea

themes.theme: Nuclear deterrence does not prevent major war

insights.claim: Macron pointed out that missiles have recently fallen on two nuclear-armed states, Russia and Israel, demonstrating that nuclear status does not sanctuarise a state's territory from major war — a lesson Guéhenno says Europe must not ignore.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: Directly undercuts the assumption that NATO's nuclear umbrella alone protects European territory from any Russian aggression — the core rationale for the article's conventional-buildup thesis.
insights.locator: A second observation in Macron’s speech

insights.claim: Ukraine's deep strikes inside Russia are teaching Putin that high-intensity war involving a nuclear-armed state can occur below the nuclear threshold — a lesson European leaders must apply to their own territory rather than assuming NATO's nuclear guarantee makes them immune to hybrid or conventional attack.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: The article's central causal claim: sub-nuclear-threshold warfare is now a demonstrated, replicable playbook that could be turned against NATO members, not just Russia.
insights.locator: Ukraine’s strikes deep inside Russia are teaching

insights.claim: If Russia's position in Ukraine keeps deteriorating over the next year, Putin may try to prove Ukraine's Western backers wrong in their belief that NATO protection makes them untouchable, using the logic that if the battlefield expands into Russia, there is no reason the war should stay confined to Ukraine's supporters' territory.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: A specific forward-looking risk scenario (12-month horizon) for escalation against NATO states tied explicitly to Russia's battlefield trajectory in Ukraine.
insights.locator: At some point in the next year

insights.claim: Latvia's intelligence service warned in late June 2026 that Russia is considering hybrid attacks — missiles, drones or other actions — designed to send the signal to stop supporting Ukraine or face domestic problems.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated intelligence warning corroborating the article's thesis that Russia is actively weighing sub-threshold coercive attacks on Ukraine's European backers.
insights.quote: hybrid attacks, such as missiles, drones or other actions designed to send a signal: stop supporting Ukraine, or you will have your own problems.
insights.locator: As Latvia’s intelligence service pointed out

insights.claim: Guéhenno dismisses the nuclear-strike threats from Russian strategist Sergey Karaganov and former president Dmitry Medvedev as bluster, distinguishing them from the more credible near-term risk of Russian hybrid or limited conventional action short of an actual NATO land incursion.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: Reframes the real near-term risk away from headline nuclear rhetoric and toward the harder-to-price hybrid/conventional threat category — useful for calibrating geopolitical-risk models.
insights.locator: One may dismiss the bluster of Russian strategist

insights.claim: It is rumoured that in late 2022, when Russia was failing in Ukraine, the US both asked China to dissuade Putin from crossing the nuclear threshold and separately conveyed that the American response would be devastating conventional strikes on Russian strategic assets — a threat Guéhenno says was far more credible than a nuclear counter-threat.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: Provides the historical precedent underpinning the article's core policy recommendation: credible conventional strike threats deter nuclear escalation better than nuclear counter-threats.
insights.locator: It is rumoured that when Russia was failing

insights.claim: Europe's future deterrence doctrine should combine long-range conventional strike capability with the nuclear deterrence needed to prevent Russian nuclear blackmail and escalation dominance, rather than treating conventional forces merely as a tripwire beneath a purely nuclear deterrent.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: The article's core policy prescription — a directional case for accelerated European investment in long-range conventional strike systems, not just nuclear signalling.
insights.locator: Deterrence is not about a division of roles

insights.claim: Britain and France, like the US, will resist being trapped into nuclear escalation triggered by an uncoordinated long-range conventional strike from a non-nuclear European power, making in-depth coordination on the escalation ladder between nuclear and non-nuclear European states a prerequisite for coherent deterrence.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific coordination requirement (nuclear vs non-nuclear power alignment) that must be solved before Europe's conventional strike build-out can function as real deterrence.
insights.locator: Britain and France, just like the US

insights.claim: The most effective near-term way to strengthen European deterrence, per Guéhenno, is accelerating and consolidating the various fragmented European programmes developing long-range strike capabilities.
insights.speaker: Jean-Marie Guéhenno
insights.so_what: A directly actionable policy conclusion — consolidation (not just funding increases) of Europe's long-range strike programmes is the recommended near-term lever.
insights.locator: the most effective way to strengthen European deterrence
